ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general dosa entry (FOODON_03311069); no specific onion dosa variant exists. Closest parent match used — the general dosa class is the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dosa — fermented rice and lentil pancake/crepe
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers dosa as a fermented cereal-legume flatbread; onion variant not separately coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dosa or onion dosa as of 2026.

preparation_method: raw onions pressed into batter during cooking

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of onion dosa is approximately 140 g (one crepe without chutneys or sambar), though restaurant portions with accompaniments may reach 200–250 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for plain dosa (fermented rice-lentil crepe). Onion dosa values are adapted from the plain dosa baseline with minor adjustments for added onion, green chili, and coriander, consistent with standard recipe proportions. Core macros and micronutrients are within the expected range for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — dosa (fermented rice-lentil crepe)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for onion dosa found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for plain dosa (fermented rice-lentil crepe) in the medium GI range (approximately 55–65), as reported in peer-reviewed literature and the University of Sydney GI Database. The addition of onion provides a small amount of dietary fibre which may marginally moderate glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 31.5) / 100 ≈ 18.3, based on 140 g default serving containing approximately 31.5 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (225 mg per 100 g); accompaniments such as sambar and chutneys can substantially increase total sodium intake
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one dosa (approximately 140 g) and choose low-sodium accompaniments, as the dosa itself contributes approximately 315 mg sodium per serving before condiments; onion provides quercetin, a flavonoid with modest vasodilatory properties.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) and moderate glycemic load per serving (~18.3); fermentation of batter may partially attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit intake to one dosa per meal, pair with high-fibre accompaniments such as sambar, and avoid high-fat chutneys; the fermented batter may offer a modest benefit to glycemic control compared with non-fermented cereal preparations.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.75 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and presence of onion-derived quercetin and other antioxidants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Onion dosa is a relatively heart-friendly choice due to its low saturated fat and cholesterol content; individuals with cardiovascular disease should use minimal oil or ghee during preparation and monitor total sodium from accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Onion and green chili contain anti-inflammatory flavonoids; fermented batter supports gut microbiome health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Onion dosa is generally well-tolerated for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the anti-inflammatory compounds in onion (quercetin) and the probiotic benefit of fermented batter may offer modest supportive effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion Dosa is a popular South Indian savory crepe made from a fermented batter of rice and urad dal (black lentil). The batter is ladled onto a hot flat griddle (tawa) and spread into a thin round crepe; finely chopped raw onions, often combined with green chilies and fresh coriander leaves, are pressed into the moist surface of the dosa while it cooks. The onions soften and lightly caramelize against the hot surface, imparting a sweet-savory flavor that contrasts with the tangy, slightly sour fermented crepe. The finished dosa is crisp at the edges and tender in the center, with a golden-brown underside. Nutritionally, onion dosa provides approximately 140 kcal per 100 g, with 4.25 g protein, 3.5 g fat, and 22.5 g carbohydrate. It is a good source of B vitamins including folate (22 mcg) and niacin (0.8 mg), and provides modest amounts of iron (0.75 mg) and potassium (155 mg). The fermentation process enhances bioavailability of minerals and may support gut health. With a medium glycemic index of approximately 58, it is suitable for most adults including those managing type 2 diabetes when portion-controlled. It is naturally gluten-free, vegetarian, and low in cholesterol. Onion dosa is commonly served at breakfast, lunch, or dinner across South India and in South Indian restaurants globally, typically accompanied by coconut chutney and sambar.
common_user_queries: How many calories in onion dosa?
Is onion dosa good for diabetes?
What is the glycemic index of dosa?

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain dosa data, which is an approved primary source for Indian foods. Onion dosa is not separately listed in ICMR-NIN; values represent a defensible adaptation from the plain dosa baseline with minor ingredient adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from published dosa GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for onion dosa is not separately reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in the primary source consulted. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): traditional onion dosa prepared with vegetable oil contains no cholesterol; when prepared with ghee, trace cholesterol may be present but is below detection threshold in standard preparations. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): this is a plant-based fermented food with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard preparation; vitamin B12 is naturally absent. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): no significant dietary vitamin D is present in the plant-based ingredients of this preparation. trans_fat_g value of 0.05 is a trace amount from possible minor processing of cooking oils; effectively negligible. Portions data from input included duplicate entries; deduplicated to three representative portions. Source 'WebMD' from input has been excluded as a forbidden source per Project Veritas v2 source policy; health suitability guidance is based on ICMR-NIN nutritional data and established clinical nutrition principles. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as the general dosa class is the nearest defensible ancestor for the onion dosa variant.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft created from input JSON. Normalized to RESULT template. Excluded forbidden source WebMD. Applied ICMR-NIN as primary source. Built full health_context with Type 2 Diabetes. Populated ontology with FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'adapted' to valid allowed values. Verified aliases consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Changed foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'adapted' (not in allowed set) to 'closest_parent' (valid allowed value per validator schema ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']). Updated gap notes to reflect closest_parent terminology. Updated notes field to document the match type rationale. All other sections preserved from iteration 2 as they passed validation.
